questions.text: Phenol when first treated with concentrated H2SO4 and then with conc. HNO3 gives:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Phenol reacts with concentrated nitric acid after being treated with concentrated sulfuric acid, resulting in the formation of 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol, commonly known as picric acid.
When phenol is treated with dilute HNO3, it can produce o-Nitrophenol; however, this is not the product formed in the reaction with concentrated acids.
Similar to o-Nitrophenol, p-Nitrophenol is formed when phenol is treated with dilute HNO3. This option does not reflect the sequence of reactions involving concentrated acids.
Nitrobenzene is the product of nitration of benzene, not phenol. Therefore, it is not relevant to the reactions of phenol with concentrated acids.

questions.explanation: Direct nitration of phenol with concentrated nitric acid can be explosive and can oxidize the ring. First treating with concentrated sulfuric acid sulfonates the ring, and subsequent treatment with nitric acid replaces the sulfonate groups with nitro groups, leading to the formation of 2,4,6- trinitrophenol (picric acid).

questions.text: For the reaction ROH + HX → R-X + H₂O, the decreasing orderof reactivity of the hydrogen halides is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Its reverse of this option.
HBr is more reactive than HCl.

This depends upon bond length. As chlorine is more electronegative than iodine so its bond length is less and stronger as compare to iodine. Similarly chlorine due to stronger bond it will also react slowly and is considered less reactive .

This depends upon bond length. As chlorine is more electronegative than iodine so its bond length is less and stronger as compare to iodine. Similarly chlorine due to stronger bond it will also react slowly and is considered less reactive .

questions.explanation: The reactivity of hydrogen halides in this reaction depends on their acidity and the strength of the H-X bond. The H-I bond is the longest and weakest, making HI the strongest acid and most reactive. The order of reactivity follows the order of bond strength: HI > HBr > HCl.

questions.text: Which of the following are the starting materials for the Grignard synthesis of tertiary butanol?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The product is 2-methyl-2-propanol, also known as tert-butyl alcohol. The Grignard reagent acts as a nucleophile, attacking the carbonyl carbon of the ketone and forming a new carbon-carbon bond, resulting in the addition of the CH3 group from the Grignard reagent to the carbonyl carbon of acetone. The oxygen in the carbonyl group becomes protonated, leading to the formation of the alcohol functional group.
In this case, CH3CH2MgBr reacts with acetaldehyde (CH3CHO). The product formed would be an alcohol, but it will be a primary alcohol (ethanol) and not tert-butyl alcohol.

The product is 2,3-dimethyl-2-butanol. In this reaction, the Grignard reagent adds to the carbonyl carbon of the alcohol, forming a new carbon-carbon bond and resulting in the incorporation of the CH3 group from the Grignard reagent into the alcohol molecule.

When a Grignard reagent reacts with a tertiary alkyl halide, it generally undergoes a nucleophilic substitution reaction. However, reactions with tertiary alkyl halides and Grignard reagents can be challenging due to steric hindrance.

questions.explanation: Grignard reagent adds to a carbonyl group to form an alcohol. To form a tertiary alcohol like tert-butanol (2- methylpropan-2-ol), a Grignard reagent must react with a ketone. The reaction of methylmagnesium bromide (CH₃MgBr) with acetone (CH₃COCH₃) yields the desired product.

questions.text: Absolute alcohol is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is correct as absolute alcohol is defined as pure ethanol, which is represented by the formula C2H5OH at 100% concentration.
This option is incorrect because CH3OH represents methanol, not ethanol. Absolute alcohol specifically refers to ethanol, not methanol.
This option is incorrect because 95% ethanol is known as rectified spirit, not absolute alcohol. Absolute alcohol is 100% ethanol.
This option is incorrect as well, as 15% ethanol is too low to be considered absolute alcohol. Absolute alcohol must be 100% ethanol.

questions.explanation: Denatured alcohol is ethanol that has been made unfit for human consumption by the addition of poisonous or foul- tasting substances. Common denaturants include methanol, pyridine, and acetone.

questions.text: The carbon to which functional group in alcohols is attached is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The carbon to which the functional group is directly attached is referred to as the alpha (α) carbon. This is a key concept in organic chemistry.
The beta (β) carbon is the carbon atom that is next to the alpha carbon; it is not the correct answer in this context.
A saturated carbon is one with all its valencies occupied, which corresponds to the alpha carbon in alcohols. This is the correct answer.
This option is incorrect because while α-carbon is relevant, only the saturated carbon directly describes the carbon's bonding characteristics in alcohols.

questions.explanation: The carbon atom directly bonded to the functional group is designated as the alpha (α) carbon. In an alcohol, this carbon atom must have four single bonds (be sp³ hybridized), making it a saturated carbon.

questions.text: Alcohol in which hydroxyl is attached to carbon which is further attached to two alkyl groups is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This type of alcohol has the hydroxyl group attached to a carbon that is bonded to only one other carbon atom.
This type of alcohol has the hydroxyl group attached to a carbon that is bonded to three other carbon atoms.
This type of alcohol has the hydroxyl group attached to a carbon that is bonded to two other carbon atoms.
This option is incorrect because the correct answer is clearly identified as a secondary alcohol.

questions.explanation: A secondary alcohol is characterized by having the hydroxyl group attached to a carbon atom that is bonded to two other carbon atoms. In contrast, a primary alcohol has the hydroxyl group on a carbon attached to only one other carbon, while a tertiary alcohol has it attached to a carbon that connects to three other carbons. Therefore, the correct choice is Secondary alcohol because it fits the description provided in the question.

questions.text: Dehydration of alcohols with conc. H2SO4 at 180•C gives:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: At high temperature, beta elimination reaction takes place and alcohols are converted to alkenes. At low temperature 140•C alcohols give ethers.
At high temperature, beta elimination reaction takes place and alcohols are converted to alkenes.
At high temperature, beta elimination reaction takes place and alcohols are converted to alkenes.
At high temperature, beta elimination reaction takes place and alcohols are converted to alkenes.

questions.explanation: At high temperature, beta elimination reaction takes place and alcohols are converted to alkenes.

questions.text: Primary alcohols upon oxidation with acified dichromate gives:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Primary alcohols upon oxidation with acified dichromate give aldehydes.
Primary alcohols upon oxidation with acified dichromate give aldehydes.
Primary alcohols upon oxidation with acified dichromate give aldehydes.
Primary alcohols upon oxidation with acified dichromate give aldehydes.

questions.explanation: The controlled oxidation of a primary alcohol with an oxidizing agent like acidified potassium dichromate will remove two hydrogen atoms to form an aldehyde. If the oxidizing agent is strong and not controlled, the aldehyde will be further oxidized to a carboxylic acid.

questions.text: Tertiary alcohols with acidified dichromate give:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Teritary alcohols do not undergo oxidation instead they undergo β -elimination to give alkenes.
Teritary alcohols do not undergo oxidation instead they undergo β -elimination to give alkenes.
Teritary alcohols do not undergo oxidation instead they undergo β -elimination to give alkenes.
Teritary alcohols do not undergo oxidation instead they undergo β -elimination to give alkenes.

questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols are resistant to oxidation because the carbon bearing the –OH group lacks a hydrogen atom. Under strongly acidic, heated conditions, they may dehydrate to form an alkene rather than undergo oxidation.

questions.text: Esterification takes place when an alcohol reacts with a(n):
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Esterification can occur when an alcohol reacts with an organic acid, and this includes carboxylic acids. The general esterification reaction involves the condensation of an alcohol and a carboxylic acid in the presence of an acid catalyst, resulting in the formation of an ester and water.
Esterification can occur when an alcohol reacts with an organic acid, and this includes carboxylic acids
Esterification can occur when an alcohol reacts with an organic acid, and this includes carboxylic acids
Esterification can occur when an alcohol reacts with an organic acid, and this includes carboxylic acids

questions.explanation: Esterification is the general process of forming an ester. This is most commonly done by reacting an alcohol with a carboxylic acid (an organic acid) but can also be achieved by reacting an alcohol with an inorganic acid, such as nitric acid or sulfuric acid.

questions.text: Which of the following is incorrect:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.text: 1₀ alcohol oxidizes to form aldehyde which is further oxidized to carboxylic acid
options.explanation: This is correct. Primary alcohols first oxidize to form aldehydes, which can further oxidize to form carboxylic acids.

options.text: 2₀ alcohol oxidizes to form ketone, which is further oxidized to carboxylic acid
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Secondary alcohols oxidize to form ketones, but ketones do not further oxidize to carboxylic acids under normal conditions.

options.text: 3° alcohol is oxidized in presence of K2Cr2O7/H2SO4 to carboxylic acids
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Tertiary alcohols do not undergo oxidation because they lack a hydrogen atom bonded to the carbon bearing the hydroxyl group.
This is incorrect because Option C is not a correct statement.

questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols do not oxidize under normal conditions because they lack the necessary hydrogen atom bonded to the carbon carrying the hydroxyl group, which is why Option C is the correct choice for the incorrect statement. Primary alcohols (1₀) oxidize first to aldehydes, which can further be oxidized to carboxylic acids, making Option A correct. Secondary alcohols (2₀) oxidize to ketones, but ketones do not further oxidize to carboxylic acids in typical conditions, rendering Option B incorrect in its claim of further oxidation.

questions.text: Alcohols react with Na metal to produce:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This product represents the alkoxide ion formed when alcohols react with sodium, as shown in the reaction: 2ROH + 2Na → 2R-ONa + H2.
This indicates the hydrogen gas released during the reaction of alcohols with sodium, as shown in the reaction: 2ROH + 2Na → 2R-ONa + H2.
This option is correct because both alkoxide ion (R-ONa) and hydrogen gas (H2) are produced in the reaction of alcohols with sodium: 2ROH + 2Na → 2R-ONa + H2.
This option is incorrect because alcohols do react with sodium metal, producing both alkoxide ions and hydrogen gas.

questions.explanation: This statement is incorrect because tertiary alcohols are resistant to oxidation as they lack a hydrogen atom on the carbon bearing the hydroxyl group.

questions.text: When ethanol reacts with sodium metal than:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option suggests that the bond between carbon and oxygen breaks. However, in the reaction with sodium metal, it is not the C—O bond that is affected. Instead, the focus is on the O—H bond in the hydroxyl group of ethanol.
This option implies the breaking of a carbon-carbon bond. In the context of the reaction between ethanol and sodium, the C—C bond remains intact during the process.

This option is correct. When ethanol reacts with sodium, the O—H bond in the hydroxyl group breaks, allowing sodium to replace the hydrogen atom, resulting in the formation of sodium ethoxide and hydrogen gas.
This option suggests that a carbon-hydrogen bond is broken. However, during the reaction with sodium, it is specifically the O—H bond in ethanol that undergoes cleavage, not the C—H bonds.

questions.explanation: In this reaction, the alcohol acts as a weak acid. The active sodium metal displaces the acidic proton from the hydroxyl group. This involves the cleavage of the polar O-H bond.

questions.text: In esterification reaction, which one of the following bonds of alcohol undergo cleavage:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The C—H bond is not involved in the esterification process, as it relates to the carbon atom's hydrogen attachment rather than the functional groups reacting.

The C—O bond is part of the structure but does not specifically correspond to the bond that is cleaved in the esterification reaction. This bond relates more to the structure of the ether or ester rather than the cleavage process.

In esterification reactions, the cleavage involves the breaking of the bond between the oxygen atom of the hydroxyl group (-OH) in the alcohol and the hydrogen atom of the carboxylic acid (or its derivative). The specific bond undergoing cleavage is the O-H bond in the alcohol.
The C—C bond is not involved in the esterification process. Its cleavage is unrelated to the reaction that forms esters.

questions.explanation: During Fischer esterification, isotopic labeling studies have confirmed that the O-H bond of the alcohol is the one that breaks. The -OH group is lost from the carboxylic acid.

questions.text: An excess of alcohol at a low temperature in the presenceof conc. H₂SO₄ produces an:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Low temperature favours formation of ethers by dehydration of alcohols, while high temprature favours β-elimination to form alkenes.
Low temperature favours formation of ethers by dehydration of alcohols, while high temprature favours β-elimination to form alkenes.
Low temperature favours formation of ethers by dehydration of alcohols, while high temprature favours β-elimination to form alkenes.
Low temperature favours formation of ethers by dehydration of alcohols, while high temprature favours β-elimination to form alkenes.

questions.explanation: The acid-catalyzed dehydration of alcohols can lead to two different products. At low temperatures (around 140°C) and with an excess of the alcohol, an intermolecular reaction (SN2 mechanism) is favored, leading to the formation of an ether.

questions.text: Phenol reacts with aqueous bromine and decolorizes aqueous bromine forming the white precipitates of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This compound is formed by the substitution of two bromine atoms on the phenol ring. However, it does not account for the complete reaction with bromine in this context.
This product contains only one bromine substituent. The reaction with aqueous bromine typically results in more extensive substitution than this option suggests.
An aqueous solution of phenol reacts with bromine water to give white ppt. of 2,4,6- tribromophenol.
This compound suggests substitution at the meta position, which is less favored in the presence of an activating group like -OH. Hence, it is not the expected product of this reaction.

questions.explanation: The -OH group of phenol is a very strong activating group for electrophilic aromatic substitution. The reaction with bromine water is rapid and goes to completion, substituting bromine atoms at all available ortho and para positions, resulting in a white precipitate of 2,4,6-tribromophenol.

questions.text: Picric acid can be prepared from phenol by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Halogenation involves the substitution of hydrogen atoms in phenol with halogen atoms. This reaction does not lead to the formation of picric acid.
Picric acid (2,4,6-trinitrophenol) can be prepared from phenol through a nitration reaction. The process involves the introduction of nitro groups (-NO2) into the phenol molecule.
Oxidation typically involves the addition of oxygen or the removal of hydrogen, which does not lead to the formation of picric acid from phenol.
Sulphonation introduces a sulfonyl group (-SO3H) into the phenol. While it can be a precursor step in the nitration process, it does not lead directly to the formation of picric acid.

questions.explanation: Picric acid is the common name for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol. It is prepared by the nitration of phenol, typically by first sulfonating the phenol and then reacting it with nitric acid.

questions.text: Phenol can be distinguished from ethanol by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Tollen's reagent is primarily used to differentiate between aldehydes and ketones, not alcohols and phenols. It does not react with either compound, making it an ineffective choice for this distinction.

Phenol reacts with bromine water, leading to the formation of a white precipitate of brominated phenol. This reaction is a key difference as alcohols do not react with bromine water in the same manner, making this a suitable test for distinguishing phenol from alcohol.

Sodium bicarbonate is used for testing carboxylic acids through effervescence. Phenol does not react significantly with NaHCO3 as it is much less acidic than carboxylic acids, while alcohols do not show a reaction either, thus it cannot be used to effectively distinguish between them.
Hydrochloric acid does not provide a clear differentiation between phenol and alcohol. Both compounds can react with acids, but HCl does not yield a definitive result to distinguish between the two.

questions.explanation: Phenol reacts with aqueous bromine to give a white precipitate, while ethanol does not react with bromine water. This provides a clear visual distinction between the two.

questions.text: What is correct order for acidity of water, phenol and ethanol:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is the correct order of acidity. Phenol's acidity is enhanced by resonance stabilization of its phenoxide ion, making it more acidic than water, while ethanol is least acidic due to the instability of its alkoxide ion.
This option incorrectly places water as more acidic than phenol, which contradicts the resonance stabilization that phenol possesses.
This option places ethanol between phenol and water, which is incorrect since water is more acidic than ethanol.
This option also incorrectly suggests that water is more acidic than phenol, which is incorrect due to phenol's resonance stabilization.

questions.explanation: Phenol (carbolic acid) is acidic and reacts with a strong base like sodium hydroxide (caustic soda) in an acid-base reaction. The products are water and the salt sodium phenoxide, which has the formula C₆H₅ONa.

questions.text: The alcohol more acidic in nature is
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alcohol that gives stable alkoxide ion are more acidic stability order of R-O-. CH3O- > C2H5O- > C3H7O- > C4H9O-

questions.text: 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol is commonly called as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Phthalic acid is a dicarboxylic acid with the molecular formula C8H6O4. It is used in the production of plastics and dyes. However, it is not the correct name for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol.
Malonic acid is another dicarboxylic acid with the molecular formula C3H4O4. It is used in organic synthesis. However, it is not the correct name for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol.
Tartaric acid is a naturally occurring organic acid commonly found in fruits. It is used in the food industry as an acidulant. However, it is not the correct name for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol.

Picric acid, also known as 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), is an aromatic compound with the molecular formula C6H3N3O7. It is a powerful explosive and yellow dye. The correct answer to this question is "d) Picric acid."

questions.explanation: Picric acid, also known as 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), is an aromatic compound with the molecular formula C6H3N3O7. It is a powerful explosive and yellow dye. The correct answer to this question is "d) Picric acid."

questions.text: Oxidation of a secondary alcohol with K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄ givesa/an:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Oxidation of a secondary alcohol with K2Cr2O7/H2SO4 does not yield an aldehyde. Instead, aldehydes are typically produced from the oxidation of primary alcohols.

This is the correct answer. The oxidation of a secondary alcohol leads to the formation of a ketone. Secondary alcohols undergo oxidation to become ketones, particularly when using strong oxidizing agents like potassium dichromate in acidic conditions.
Carboxylic acids are generally the result of the oxidation of primary alcohols or aldehydes, not secondary alcohols. Therefore, this option is incorrect in the context of the given reaction.

Ethers are not produced through the oxidation of alcohols. Instead, they are formed through the reaction of alcohols with alkyl halides. Thus, this option is incorrect in the context of the oxidation of a secondary alcohol.

questions.explanation: Strong oxidizing agents, such as acidified potassium dichromate, will oxidize a secondary alcohol to a ketone. The reaction stops at the ketone stage as there are no hydrogens left on the carbonyl carbon for further oxidation.

questions.text: Which is most acidic?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Water is more acidic than ethanol because the oxygen atom in water has two lone pairs of electrons, which can be donated to a proton. Ethanol only has one lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom, so it is less acidic. Isopropyl alcohol and n-propyl alcohol are both primary alcohols, and they have the same acidity. The acidity of an alcohol is determined by the strength of the O-H bond. The stronger the O-H bond, the less acidic the alcohol. The O-H bond in water is the strongest of the four alcohols, so water is the most acidic. The acidity of an alcohol can also be affected by the electronegativity of the alkyl group attached to the oxygen atom. The more electronegative the alkyl group, the weaker the O-H bond and the more acidic the alcohol. In the four alcohols given, the alkyl groups are all methyl groups, so they have the same electronegativity.

questions.text: Alcohols of low molecular weight are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alcohols of low molecular weight are soluble in water. This is because the alcohols can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules. Hydrogen bonds are attractions between a hydrogen atom that is bonded to a highly electronegative atom, such as oxygen, and a lone pair of electrons on another atom. The hydroxyl group in an alcohol contains an oxygen atom that is bonded to a hydrogen atom. This oxygen atom is highly electronegative, so it can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules. The water molecules can then form hydrogen bonds with each other, which results in the alcohol being soluble in water. As the molecular weight of the alcohol increases, the solubility in water decreases. This is because the hydrocarbon chain on the alcohol becomes more hydrophobic, which means that it does not like to interact with water. The hydrophobic part of the alcohol prevents it from forming hydrogen bonds with water molecules, which reduces its solubility in water.
Alcohols of low molecular weight are soluble in water while that of high molecular weight are insoluble in water .
Alcohols of low molecular weight are soluble in water while that of high molecular weight are insoluble in water.
Alcohols of low molecular weight are soluble in water while that of high molecular weight are insoluble in water.

questions.explanation: Alcohols of low molecular weight are soluble in water. This is because the alcohols can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules. The hydroxyl group in an alcohol contains an oxygen atom that is bonded to a hydrogen atom. This oxygen atom is highly electronegative, so it can form hydrogen bonds with water molecules. The water molecules can then form hydrogen bonds with each other, which results in the alcohol being soluble in water. As the molecular weight of the alcohol increases, the solubility in water decreases. This is because the hydrocarbon chain on the alcohol becomes more hydrophobic, which means that it does not like to interact with water. The hydrophobic part of the alcohol prevents it from forming hydrogen bonds with water molecules, which reduces its solubility in water.

questions.text: Which of the following is the weakest acid:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Phenol is a weak acid because the hydroxyl group is not as acidic as the carboxylic acid group. The hydroxyl group in phenol is attached to an aromatic ring, which makes it less acidic. The aromatic ring is electron-withdrawing, which pulls electrons away from the hydroxyl group and makes it less likely to donate a proton.

Carboxylic acids are the strongest acids of the four given acids. The carboxylic acid group is composed of a carbon atom that is double-bonded to an oxygen atom and is also bonded to an hydroxyl group. The double bond between the carbon atom and the oxygen atom makes the carboxylic acid group very polar, which means that the electrons are not evenly distributed. The oxygen atom has a higher electronegativity than the carbon atom, so it has a partial negative charge. The hydroxyl group has a partial positive charge. This polarity makes it easy for the carboxylic acid group to donate a proton.

Alcohols are slightly acidic because the hydroxyl group can donate a proton. However, the hydroxyl group in an alcohol is not as acidic as the carboxylic acid group or the phenol group. The hydroxyl group in an alcohol is not double-bonded to an oxygen atom. Alcohols are the weaker among the given options.
Water is a very weak acid because the hydroxyl group in water is very stable. However, it is stronger than alcohol, because it does not contain any alkyl group.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. Alcohols are weaker than all other given compounds. The trend of acidity is; Carboxylic acids>Phenols>Water>Alcohols.

questions.text: Which of the following is more reactive where O-H bonds break:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Primary (P°) alcohols are more reactive where the O-H bonds break because the steric hindrance around the carbon atom that is bonded to the hydroxyl group is the least. This means that the hydrogen atom on the hydroxyl group is more easily removed.
Ternary (T°) alcohols have two methyl groups on the carbon atom that is bonded to the hydroxyl group. This makes it even more difficult for the hydrogen atom on the hydroxyl group to be removed.

Secondary (S°) alcohols have a methyl group on the carbon atom that is bonded to the hydroxyl group. This methyl group increases the steric hindrance around the carbon atom, making it more difficult for the hydrogen atom on the hydroxyl group to be removed.

Primary (P°) alcohols are more reactive where the O-H bonds break because the steric hindrance around the carbon atom that is bonded to the hydroxyl group is the least. This means that the hydrogen atom on the hydroxyl group is more easily removePrimary (P°) alcohols are more reactive where the O-H bonds break because the steric hindrance around the carbon atom that is bonded to the hydroxyl group is the least. This means that the hydrogen atom on the hydroxyl group is more easily removed.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct. Primary (P°) alcohols are more reactive where the O-H bonds break because the steric hindrance around the carbon atom that is bonded to the hydroxyl group is the least. This means that the hydrogen atom on the hydroxyl group is more easily removed.

questions.text: Ethanol reacts with sodium metal to liberate:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The reaction mechanism between ethanol and sodium is as follows: The formation of hydrogen gas bubbles and Sodium ethoxide is formed with a colorless solution. 2 CH 3 CH 2 OH ( l ) Ethanol + 2 Na ( s Sodium ) → 2 CH 3 CH 2 ONa ( l ) Sodium ethoxide + H 2 ( g ) Hydrogen.The reaction mechanism between ethanol and sodium is as follows: The formation of hydrogen gas bubbles and Sodium ethoxide is formed with a colorless solution. 2 CH 3 CH 2 OH ( l ) Ethanol + 2 Na ( s Sodium ) → 2 CH 3 CH 2 ONa ( l ) Sodium ethoxide + H 2 ( g ) Hydrogen.

The reaction mechanism between ethanol and sodium is as follows:The formation of hydrogen gas bubbles and Sodium ethoxide is formed with a colorless solution. 2 CH 3 CH 2 OH ( l ) Ethanol + 2 Na ( s Sodium ) → 2 CH 3 CH 2 ONa ( l ) Sodium ethoxide + H 2 ( g ) Hydrogen.

Yes, ethanol reacts with sodium metal to liberate hydrogen gas. The reaction can be represented by the following equation: 2CH3CH2OH + 2Na → 2CH3CH2ONa + H2 In this reaction, the sodium metal donates electrons to the hydroxyl group of the ethanol molecule, forming a sodium ethoxide molecule and hydrogen gas. The hydrogen gas is released as bubbles. The reaction is exothermic, meaning that it releases heat. The heat from the reaction can cause the ethanol to boil and vaporize. The reaction is also very exothermic, so it is important to be careful when performing it. The heat from the reaction can cause the sodium metal to ignite, so it is important to do the reaction in a well-ventilated area.

The reaction mechanism between ethanol and sodium is as follows:The formation of hydrogen gas bubbles and Sodium ethoxide is formed with a colorless solution. 2 CH 3 CH 2 OH ( l ) Ethanol + 2 Na ( s Sodium ) → 2 CH 3 CH 2 ONa ( l ) Sodium ethoxide + H 2 ( g ) Hydrogen.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.Yes, ethanol reacts with sodium metal to liberate hydrogen gas. The reaction can be represented by the following equation: 2CH3CH2OH + 2Na → 2CH3CH2ONa + H2 In this reaction, the sodium metal donates electrons to the hydroxyl group of the ethanol molecule, forming a sodium ethoxide molecule and hydrogen gas. The hydrogen gas is released as bubbles. The reaction is exothermic, meaning that it releases heat. The heat from the reaction can cause the ethanol to boil and vaporize. The reaction is also very exothermic, so it is important to be careful when performing it. The heat from the reaction can cause the sodium metal to ignite, so it is important to do the reaction in a well-ventilated area.

questions.explanation: The order of reactivity with respect to O-H bonds is CH3OH> primary alcohol >secondary alcohol >tertiary alcohol.

questions.text: The number of resonating structures of phenoxide ion are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A suggests that there are three resonating structures for the phenoxide ion.
Option B states that there are five resonating structures for the phenoxide ion.
Option C claims that there are four resonating structures for the phenoxide ion.
Option D indicates that there are six resonating structures for the phenoxide ion.

questions.explanation: The correct option for the number of resonating structures of the phenoxide ion is B) 5. The phenoxide ion (C6H5O-) exhibits resonance due to the delocalization of electrons across the aromatic ring, leading to the existence of five resonating structures. Resonance structures play a crucial role in stabilizing the phenoxide ion and contribute to its overall reactivity in various chemical reactions.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the isomer of dimethyl ether?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option represents ethanol, which is the isomer of dimethyl ether. Both compounds have the molecular formula C2H6O but differ in their structure. Ethanol has an -OH group, whereas dimethyl ether has an oxygen atom connecting two methyl groups.
This option represents acetaldehyde, which is not an isomer of dimethyl ether. Acetaldehyde has a carbonyl group (C=O) and does not share the same molecular formula as dimethyl ether.
This option represents acetic acid. It is not an isomer of dimethyl ether because it contains a carboxylic acid group (-COOH) and has a different molecular formula.
This option represents methyl formate. It is not an isomer of dimethyl ether because it contains a formate group (-HCOO) and has a different molecular formula.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is CH3CH2OH, which is ethanol. Ethanol and dimethyl ether are isomers because they share the same molecular formula, C2H6O, but differ in their structural arrangement. Ethanol has a hydroxyl (-OH) group, whereas dimethyl ether has an oxygen atom bridging two methyl groups. The other options, CH3CHO (acetaldehyde), CH3COOH (acetic acid), and HCOOCH3 (methyl formate), have different molecular formulas or functional groups and are not isomers of dimethyl ether.

questions.text: Esterification of CH3COOH is_reaction.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Esterification is not an acid-base reaction. Esterification is the process of combining an organic acid (RCOOH) with an alcohol (ROH) to form an ester (RCOOR) and water; or a chemical reaction resulting in the formation of at least one ester product.
Since there is no change in the oxidation state of the atoms involved, esterification is not a redox reaction.
Electrophilic reactions involve the attack of an electron-rich species on an electron-deficient center. Esterification involves nucleophilic attack.

Esterification is a nucleophilic substitution reaction. Esterification is the process of combining an organic acid (RCOOH) with an alcohol (ROH) to form an ester (RCOOR) and water; or a chemical reaction resulting in the formation of at least one ester product.

questions.explanation: Esterification is a nucleophilic substitution reaction. Esterification is the process of combining an organic acid (RCOOH) with an alcohol (ROH) to form an ester (RCOOR) and water; or a chemical reaction resulting in the formation of at least one ester product.

questions.text: The compound that reacts the slowest in Lucas test.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Since 1-Pentanol is a primary alcohol it will react the slowest with the Lucas reagent.
3-Pentanol is a secondary alcohol it will react quickly with the Lucas test as compared to the primary alcohol. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Sec-butyl alcohol is a secondary alcohol it will react quickly with the Lucas test as compared to the primary alcohol. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Ter-butyl alcohol is a tertiary alcohol it will react quickly with the Lucas test as compared to the primary or secondary alcohol. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Lucas test is used to distinguish between alcohols. Tertiary alcohols react the fastest, followed by the secondary alcohols and the primary alcohols react the slowest. Since 1-Pentanol is a primary alcohol it will react the slowest with the Lucas reagent.

questions.text: An electron withdrawing-group attached to the o-position in phenol:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Phenol itself is a weak acid due to the presence of the hydroxyl group (-OH), which can donate a proton (H+) to water to some extent. However, adding an electron-withdrawing group to the ortho position (o-position) would actually decrease the basicity of phenol, as it would withdraw electron density from the oxygen, making it less likely to donate a proton.

Electron-withdrawing groups, as the name suggests, withdraw electron density from the phenolic oxygen. This electron withdrawal reduces the availability of the lone pair of electrons on oxygen, making it less willing to donate a proton (H+) and increasing the acidity of phenol.

When phenol loses a proton (H+) in the presence of a base, it forms a phenoxide ion (C6H5O-). The electron-withdrawing group at the ortho position would stabilize the negative charge on the phenoxide ion by delocalizing the negative charge through resonance. This resonance stabilization makes the phenoxide ion more stable compared to a phenoxide ion without an electron-withdrawing group.

options.text: allows it to precipitate in an aqueous solution
options.explanation: The presence of an electron-withdrawing group at the ortho position does not have a direct effect on the solubility of phenol or its phenoxide ion in water. The solubility of phenol in water is primarily determined by the presence of the hydroxyl group (-OH), which allows it to form hydrogen bonds with water molecules.

questions.explanation: e- withdrawing groups stabilize negative charge.

-ing makes the verb in its present continuous form. Therefore it's the right choice here.

questions.text: Statement:The Management of School M has decided to give free breakfast from next academic year to all the students in its primary section through its canteen even though they will not get any government grant.Courses of Action(I) The school will have to admit many poor students who will seek admission for the next academic year.(II) The canteen facilities and utensils have to be checked and new purchases to be made to equip it properly.(III) Funds will have to be raised to support the sc heme for years to come.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Since the school has introduced the scheme without receiving any grant, it needs to pool up the funds and make adequate arrangements to keep the scheme going. So, only II and III follow.

Option B is incorrect because only conclusion I follows. The statement mentions that the school will provide free breakfast to all students in the primary section; there is no indication about admitting many poor students.

Option C is incorrect because only conclusion I and II follow. The statement supports the provision of free breakfast and the need for checking canteen facilities and utensils, but it doesn't specifically mention raising funds.

Option D is incorrect because both conclusion I and II follow. The statement clearly states that the school management decided to provide free breakfast to all students in the primary section, which may lead to admitting more students, and it implies the need for checking canteen facilities. However, there is no direct mention of raising funds.

questions.explanation: Since the school has introduced the scheme without receiving any grant, it needs to pool up the funds and make adequate arrangements to keep the scheme going. So, only II and III follow.

questions.text: Statement:The availability of imported fruits has increased in the indigenous market and so the demand for indigenous fruits has been decreased.Course of Action:I. To help the indigenous producers of fruits, the Government should impose high import duty on these fruits, even if these are not of good quality.II. The fruit vendors should stop selling imported fruits. So that the demand for indigenous fruits, would be increased.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because both course of actions are not valid. Imposing high import duty on low-quality fruits may not necessarily help indigenous producers, and it could lead to adverse effects on consumers.

The ideas suggested in both I and II represent unfair means to cut competition. The correct way would be to devise methods and techniques such that the indigenous producers could produce better quality fruits and make them available in the market at prices comparable with those of the imported ones. Hence, neither I nor II follows.
Option C is incorrect because only course of action I is not valid. Imposing high import duty on low-quality fruits without considering the quality aspect can have negative consequences.

Option D is incorrect because only course of action II is not valid. Stopping the sale of imported fruits might not necessarily increase the demand for indigenous fruits, as consumer preferences and choices vary.

questions.explanation: The ideas suggested in both I and II represent unfair means to cut competition. The correct way would be to devise methods and techniques such that the indigenous producers could produce better quality fruits and make them available in the market at prices comparable with those of the imported ones. Hence, neither I nor II follows.

questions.text: Fact l: All drink mixes are beveragesFact 2: All beverages are drinkableFact 3: All beverages are redIf the above three statements are facts then which of the following statement will also be a fact?I. Some drink mixes are redII. All beverages are drink mixesIII. All red drink mixes are drinkable
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because there is no direct link between drink mixes and being red in the given facts. While all beverages are red, it doesn't mean that all drink mixes are red.
Option B is incorrect because the fact states that all drink mixes are beverages, not vice versa. So, it cannot be concluded that all beverages are drink mixes.

Drink mixes may or may not be red, so option I is not a fact. All drink mixes are beverages but beverages can also be other than drink mixes, option II is also not a fact. Because all drink mixes are beverages and all beverages are drinkable, therefore all red drink mixes will be drinkable. Option III is a fact.

options.text: None of them is a fact
options.explanation: This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Drink mixes may or may not be red, so option I is not a fact. All drink mixes are beverages but beverages can also be other than drink mixes, option II is also not a fact. Because all drink mixes are beverages and all beverages are drinkable, therefore all red drink mixes will be drinkable. Option III is a fact.
